name: scarce wand of calming
shortDescription: A rare wand said to soften panic, quiet quarrels, and make tense rooms remember how to breathe. Healers, negotiators, and caravan masters value it highly, though wise buyers test it on tempers before trusting it with disaster.
flavorText: “Peace is worth more when it fits in one hand.”

name: wand of knowledge
shortDescription: A studious wand associated with memory, insight, and the dangerous habit of asking better questions. Scribes and scholars prize it for research, while merchants know any tool promising wisdom can be sold twice: once to the clever and once to the hopeful.
flavorText: “It does not make fools wise. It makes them easier to notice.”

name: wand of air
shortDescription: A light wand that hums with drafts, fluttering dust and loose parchment whenever its case is opened. Sailors, messengers, stage magicians, and weather-minded hedge mages all ask after it, especially when roads are still and skies are stubborn.
flavorText: “The breeze obeys briefly, then remembers it is free.”

name: wand of life
shortDescription: A warm-toned wand linked to vigor, growth, and the stubborn return of green things after winter. Gardeners call it blessed, physicians call it interesting, and cautious guilds call it expensive enough to keep locked away until needed.
flavorText: “Life answers softly, but it does answer.”

name: scarce wand of power
shortDescription: A scarce wand with a dense, forceful presence, the sort that makes apprentices stand straighter and masters ask where it was found. It is not subtle stock; buyers come for strength, reputation, and the thrill of holding more certainty than sense.
flavorText: “Power has a weight even when carved thin.”

name: scarce wand of fire
shortDescription: A rare fire-aspected wand with a warm grip and a faint scent of smoke around the grain. Furnace keepers, battle mages, and wealthy collectors all want one, though responsible merchants store it away from oil, straw, and optimism.
flavorText: “Flame is a loyal servant only in stories told by the unburned.”

name: scarce wand of light
shortDescription: A scarce wand that gathers brightness along its tip even in dim rooms, favored by pilgrims, explorers, and anyone tired of paying torchbearers. Its charm is practical as well as holy-looking, which makes temples and mine captains unlikely rivals at auction.
flavorText: “Light is the cheapest miracle until darkness gets organized.”

name: market Map
shortDescription: A common market map marked with streets, stalls, wells, warehouse lanes, and the routes polite visitors are supposed to use. It is less adventurous than a border chart, but far more useful to traders trying to find profit before closing bells.
flavorText: “A good market map saves coin by preventing confident wandering.”

name: scarce Map of the War
shortDescription: A scarce wartime map showing old fronts, burned crossings, supply roads, and places where ink still feels nervous. Collectors prize it, veterans mistrust it, and merchants study it for the same reason generals did: routes decide everything.
flavorText: “The lines are quiet now. That does not make them harmless.”

name: Map of the West
shortDescription: A western route map folded hard at the corners and annotated with towns, passes, wells, and several warnings written by someone practical. Caravans buy it before long journeys, while innkeepers sell copies to travelers who suddenly realize west is not a plan.
flavorText: “West is a direction until the road starts charging for details.”

name: Map of Border March
shortDescription: A borderland map of watch roads, toll points, river crossings, and settlements that change allegiance faster than ink dries. It is useful to merchants, scouts, and cautious smugglers, though everyone agrees the margins should have been larger for new warnings.
flavorText: “In the March, a map is never wrong for long.”

name: fabled Map To Brasskeep
shortDescription: A fabled map said to mark the safest path to Brasskeep, though safest is a word mapmakers use more bravely than travelers. Its routes are drawn in confident ink and guarded by rumor, making it valuable even to those who never intend to go.
flavorText: “Every road to Brasskeep asks what you brought for the gates.”

name: greater reef serpent
shortDescription: A greater reef serpent specimen or contract-token, representing one of the larger scaled dangers claimed from coral waters. Apothecaries want glands, nobles want trophies, and sailors want proof the thing is nowhere near their hulls anymore.
flavorText: “The reef grows teeth when the tide is hungry.”

name: guardian reef serpent
shortDescription: A guardian reef serpent, prized less for convenience than for the awe attached to its name. Coastal shrines, monster brokers, and trophy buyers all argue over such stock, because anything called guardian suggests both danger and a story worth charging for.
flavorText: “Some creatures guard treasure. Others make the reef itself the treasure.”

name: scarce hydrataur
shortDescription: A scarce hydrataur specimen, rare enough that even experienced coastal traders lower their voices when discussing price. Its parts, sketches, or capture rights attract scholars and warlords alike, though transport costs rise whenever the cargo is still damp and angry.
flavorText: “Half legend, half tide, and entirely bad for cages.”

name: tidefolk aquamancer
shortDescription: A tidefolk aquamancer token or hire writ, representing a water-worker whose skill can bless wells, calm channels, or ruin a careless harbor. Such contracts are valuable near coasts, though every clause should specify exactly who commands the waves and when.
flavorText: “Water listens best to those who never call it tame.”

name: tidefolk avatar
shortDescription: A tidefolk avatar writ, effigy, or rare encounter claim tied to one of the sea-born figures treated with reverence along the coast. Temples, collectors, and diplomats may all seek it, but none agree whether ownership is sensible or sacrilege.
flavorText: “The sea does not kneel; sometimes it simply chooses a face.”

name: tidefolk commoner
shortDescription: A tidefolk commoner contract-token or record, modest beside warrior writs but valuable to anyone trading with reef settlements. Common hands mend nets, carry news, translate customs, and remind inland merchants that ordinary labor is often the rarest bridge.
flavorText: “Every strange nation is mostly people buying supper.”

name: tidefolk fighter
shortDescription: A tidefolk fighter writ or unit token, associated with disciplined coastal defenders used to surf, spearwork, and sudden weather. Mercenary captains buy such services carefully, because tidefolk fighters know the shoreline better than most maps ever will.
flavorText: “On land they fight. In water they arrive.”

name: tidefolk impaler
shortDescription: A tidefolk impaler token, representing a spear-bearing specialist whose reputation travels ahead of the tide. Coastal fortresses and private guards may pay for such service, though the name alone is usually enough to make caravan accountants swallow twice.
flavorText: “Some warnings are written on banners. Others carry spears.”

name: tidefolk javelineer
shortDescription: A tidefolk javelineer contract, valued by captains who need accurate throws from decks, docks, or slippery stones. Their gear is light, their range useful, and their pay negotiable only until someone remembers how hard it is to hire skill from the sea.
flavorText: “A javelin crosses the distance before pride can.”

name: fishwoman
shortDescription: A fishwoman encounter writ or coastal token, traded among scholars, diplomats, and markets that traffic in the strange edge between people and tide. Such records fetch better prices when respectful, because reef settlements remember insults longer than invoices.
flavorText: “Half the trade is language; the other half is not staring.”

name: fishman
shortDescription: A fishman token or hire note from the wet borderlands, useful to collectors, coastal brokers, and captains needing guides through reefs and river mouths. Inland buyers often underestimate the value until the first submerged road proves them foolish.
flavorText: “A fishman knows where maps give up and currents begin.”

name: reef serpent
shortDescription: A reef serpent specimen, scale bundle, or bounty proof from coral channels where bright water hides sharp movement. Alchemists want the material, sailors want the warning, and collectors want the story polished until it sounds almost safe.
flavorText: “The reef is beautiful because it can afford camouflage.”

name: reef serpent mage
shortDescription: A reef serpent mage writ, token, or record tied to spell-casting serpentfolk of the coastal deeps. Its value rises among arcanists and diplomats alike, especially when accompanied by proof that the magic was observed from a survivable distance.
flavorText: “Scales and spells make poor cargo, but excellent rumors.”

name: reef serpent ritualist
shortDescription: A reef serpent ritualist token associated with tide rites, coral shrines, and magic performed where land folk should not interrupt. Temples and scholars pay carefully for such records, while coastal elders advise against owning what one does not understand.
flavorText: “Some rituals are best priced from the shore.”

name: reef serpent sharpshooter
shortDescription: A reef serpent sharpshooter contract-token, denoting a precise coastal fighter reputed to strike from cover, foam, or broken reef. Military buyers like the skill; merchants like the intimidation; everyone dislikes imagining how the target first learned the price.
flavorText: “Accuracy is more frightening when the water hides the hand.”

name: reef serpent warrior
shortDescription: A reef serpent warrior token or trophy writ linked to hardened fighters of the coral channels. Such goods sell to collectors and mercenary brokers, though the wisest appraisers ask whether the warrior's kin consider the matter settled.
flavorText: “A warrior from the reef brings the tide's patience and its teeth.”

name: reef serpent spiritualist
shortDescription: A reef serpent spiritualist record or ceremonial token connected to serpentfolk rites, ancestor currents, and deep-water interpretation. It is a delicate trade good: valuable to the right scholar, offensive to the wrong clan, and never merely decorative.
flavorText: “The spirits of the reef speak in bubbles and consequences.”

name: tidefolk siren
shortDescription: A tidefolk siren token, song writ, or diplomatic record bound to voices sailors describe with fear and longing in equal parts. Performance houses, courts, and naval officers may all bid, though none admit how much of the price is curiosity.
flavorText: “The song is never free; only the first note sounds generous.”

name: water nymph
shortDescription: A water nymph token or encounter proof, associated with springs, pools, and beautiful trouble near old forests. It draws interest from poets, priests, and collectors of rare beings, but experienced merchants keep the paperwork dry and the promises vague.
flavorText: “Clear water often hides the most complicated bargains.”

name: anubis
shortDescription: An anubis figure, token, or encounter claim tied to jackal-headed guardians and desert rites of judgment. It sells best to temple collectors and scholars of old tomb roads, though handlers treat the name with a caution ordinary statues do not receive.
flavorText: “Some guardians watch gates. Some watch what follows after.”

name: basilisk
shortDescription: A basilisk specimen or bounty proof from the sort of creature buyers inspect through smoked glass and professional distance. Its eyes, scales, and rumors all have value, but every merchant learns quickly that fear is part of the packaging.
flavorText: “Never haggle with anything people are afraid to look at.”

name: big kobold
shortDescription: A big kobold token, hire writ, or bounty mark representing the larger, meaner end of an already troublesome breed. Mine owners, dungeon brokers, and militia clerks all know the type: small enough to underestimate, large enough to regret it.
flavorText: “Kobolds grow into exactly as much problem as the tunnel allows.”

name: centaur warrior
shortDescription: A centaur warrior contract-token, prized by frontier lords and caravan captains needing speed, strength, and a fighter who carries their own cavalry with them. The hiring price is high, but so is the cost of explaining insults to a centaur band.
flavorText: “A centaur warrior arrives with hooves, bow, and opinions.”

name: centaur
shortDescription: A centaur record or travel writ, valued by diplomats, scouts, and merchants working the grass roads where horse and human customs meet uneasily. Such dealings demand careful gifts, broad paths, and enough respect to keep negotiations from galloping away.
flavorText: “Half horse, half human, entirely aware when you stare.”

name: centaur leader
shortDescription: A centaur leader writ or token tied to authority among fast-moving plains folk. It is valuable in diplomacy and dangerous in forgery, since a leader's mark can open pasture roads, settle disputes, or start the kind that hooves finish.
flavorText: “The herd follows the one who knows both road and storm.”

name: death yak
shortDescription: A death yak bounty proof or specimen tag from a beast whose name alone saves merchants time in explanation. Its horns, hide, and stories attract grim collectors, but drovers insist it should be transported only after everyone is completely certain it is done objecting.
flavorText: “Some livestock becomes legendary by refusing the category.”

name: deathcap
shortDescription: A deathcap specimen, carefully sealed and labeled because attractive mushrooms are often the worst liars. Poisoners, alchemists, and cautious herbalists all know its worth, while honest cooks prefer it remain a lesson rather than an ingredient.
flavorText: “Pretty fungi have ended uglier arguments than swords.”

name: demonic bear
shortDescription: A demonic bear token or trophy claim, carrying the reputation of claws, corrupted hide, and a temperament no sane hunter calls natural. Collectors pay for menace, wardens pay for proof, and tanners usually decline unless paid in advance.
flavorText: “Regular bears are warnings. This one is punctuation.”

name: demonic elephant
shortDescription: A demonic elephant writ, tusk claim, or campaign trophy tied to an enormous corrupted beast few caravans survive meeting. The material value is high, but the story value is higher, especially when every witness agrees from a safe distance.
flavorText: “When something that large turns wicked, geography notices.”

name: scarce fire drake
shortDescription: A scarce fire drake specimen claim, scale bundle, or bounty mark from a creature hot enough to make handlers argue over gloves. Smiths, mages, and collectors all bid fiercely, while warehouses charge extra for the privilege of not burning down.
flavorText: “A fire drake leaves warmth, ash, and inflated insurance.”

name: formicid
shortDescription: A formicid token representing an antlike humanoid of tunnels, colonies, and unsettling discipline. Scholars want records, mine lords want warnings, and military buyers quietly admire any people who can organize underground better than most cities do above it.
flavorText: “One formicid is a curiosity. Several become logistics.”

name: venomous formicid
shortDescription: A venomous formicid specimen claim or warning token, rarer and more valuable than its common kin because danger improves both price and paperwork. Apothecaries ask about venom glands; guards ask whether the container was checked twice.
flavorText: “Venom makes a tunnel negotiation shorter.”

name: gnoll
shortDescription: A gnoll token, bounty proof, or mercenary record from the rough border markets where toothy raiders and hired muscle are sometimes separated only by contract wording. Practical buyers ask about loyalty; experienced ones ask how recently it ate.
flavorText: “A gnoll bargain often begins with someone counting fingers.”

name: bogling
shortDescription: A bogling specimen tag or marshland bounty proof, small enough to sound harmless until farmers start listing losses. Alchemists may want slime, local councils want relief, and merchants want the jar sealed before anyone says it looks cute.
flavorText: “Every swamp has mischief with teeth.”

name: massive guardian serpent
shortDescription: A massive guardian serpent claim from old ruins, temple roads, or deep forests where something enormous was left to remember a boundary. Its scales and story sell well, but wise traders ask what it was guarding before touching the prize.
flavorText: “A guardian that big suggests a secret worth leaving alone.”

name: hell hog
shortDescription: A hell hog bounty mark or trophy piece from a bristling creature that gives farmers nightmares and hunters better scars. Its tusks, hide, and unpleasant legend have market value, though no butcher advertises the meat without a disclaimer.
flavorText: “Some pigs find truffles. Some find reasons to charge danger pay.”

name: hell hound
shortDescription: A hell hound token or bounty proof, dark with heat, smoke, and the kind of reputation that clears tavern tables when mentioned aloud. Wardens pay for proof of one destroyed; warlocks and collectors ask much less comforting questions.
flavorText: “Bad dogs bite. Hell hounds leave witnesses smelling of smoke.”

name: bridge bogling
shortDescription: A bridge bogling claim, usually tied to the damp, rude creatures that haunt old crossings and make tolls feel reasonable by comparison. Local merchants value proof of removal, since nothing hurts trade like cargo stopped by a nuisance with claws and a favorite bridge.
flavorText: “Every bridge has a cost. Some demand coin; some demand patience.”
